deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turns 1–10; Transcript 2, Turns 1–10; Transcript 3, Turns 1–10
deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—e.g., why building the student's own reasoning matters for learning, retention, or skill development. The tutoring constraints are enforced consistently but the rationale for those constraints is never articulated to the student.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turns 1–10; Transcript 2, Turns 1–10; Transcript 3, Turns 1–10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful progress (e.g., T1 Turn 7 student correctly identifies coastal flooding and extreme heat; T2 Turn 8 student produces strong reasoning; T3 Turn 8 student drafts a solid justification), the tutor moves forward without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the improved approach worked.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly asks for a broader explanation or a finished example across multiple turns (Turns 3, 5, 6, 8), and the tutor cycles through similar reframings of the same structure (stressor → sector → rating → justification) without meaningfully consolidating or advancing. The student remains stuck in the same conceptual loop for six turns without the tutor switching to a substantially different strategy such as a concrete worked analogy or a direct reframe of the student's anxiety.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly on Socratic method and assignment anchoring across all three transcripts, never giving submission-ready answers and consistently keeping reasoning student-led. Scaffolding and progression are generally solid, with the tutor building on student contributions. The main weaknesses are: (1) a persistent spiral in Transcript 1 where the student's anxiety about structure is met with repeated reframings of the same framework rather than a genuinely different intervention; (2) the tutoring rationale is never explained to students across any transcript; and (3) moments of student improvement are not followed by reflection prompts. Communication is clear and appropriately bite-sized throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to generate key ideas before confirming them. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on student progress in each turn and uses diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained (1.3.A.a) and no reflection is prompted after student improvement in any of the three transcripts (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points for Transcript 1, where the student spirals across six turns with the same structural anxiety and the tutor repeats the same framework reframing without switching to a meaningfully different strategy (e.g., a concrete analogy, a direct acknowledgment of the anxiety as separate from the task, or a suggestion to just attempt a draft and iterate). 2.2 and all communication criteria receive full marks, as the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses focused, and maintains a supportive tone throughout.
